summary: index_information fails on collection names containing periods
description:
Lines 448, 449 of db.rb:

            h[:ns].sub!(/.*\./, '')
            raise "Error: ns != collection" unless h[:ns] == collection_name

I'm not sure of the history of the sub! call but it doesn't seem necessary given the availability of full_coll_name().  Changing the raise to:

            raise "Error: ns != collection" unless h[:ns] == sel[:ns]

works. :)

BTW, for one liner patches do you prefer a patch, Github fork/pull, or...?

summary: OrderedHash initialize does not work for inserting objects.  Only using Hash initialize method works
description:
This ruby script shows the problem well.
http://gist.github.com/167472

When you create an OrderedHash via the initialize method, the attributes are not inserted into the db.  This bug does not occur if you create the OrderedHash and separately add key/value pairs.  It also works if you use Hash, either with initializer or without.

summary: The :left server has to be the master.
description:
I started a replica pair with server s1 and s2. The server s2 becomes the master. 
With the Ruby driver, I set :left to s1 and :right to s2. But the driver raises an exception, saying that s1 is not a master. 

Should the driver check the other server to see if this one is the master ? 

Here is the code I used:

XGen::Mongo::Driver::Mongo.new({:left => ["127.0.0.1", 27017], :right => ["127.0.0.1", 27018]}, nil, :auto_reconnect => true)

summary: find with partial document :fields supplied containing a dot not returning proper results
description:
The behavior of find with :fields in the ruby library to request a partial document is inconsistent with what's returned in the mongo shell.

In the shell, let's create 2 documents:
db.test.save({a1: {b1: {c1: 10} } })
db.test.save({a1: {b2: {c1: 20} } })

Now, let's ask it only for the documents starting at a1.b1 :
> db.test.find({}, {"a1.b1": 1})
{"_id" :  ObjectId( "4a96950b09f1193462417fd0")  , "a1" : {"b1" : {"c1" : 10} , "b1" : {"c1" : 10}}}
{"_id" :  ObjectId( "4a96953a09f1193462417fd1")  , "a1" : {}}

Something's a bit weird.  Notice how the "b1" key is repeated in the first document.  Not a huge deal.
The second document is as expected since it lacks a "b1" key.

Now let's use ruby to ask for the same partial document:

require "rubygems"
require "mongo"
test_db = Mongo::Connection.new.db("test")
test_coll = test_db.collection("test")
test_coll.find({}, :fields => "a1.b1").each do |doc|
  puts doc.inspect
end

prints:
{"_id"=>#<Mongo::ObjectID:0x118dafc @data=[74, 150, 149, 11, 9, 241, 25, 52, 98, 65, 127, 208]>, "a1"=>{"b1"=>{}}}
{"_id"=>#<Mongo::ObjectID:0x118d660 @data=[74, 150, 149, 58, 9, 241, 25, 52, 98, 65, 127, 209]>, "a1"=>{}}

Notice the first record.  It's reporting that "b1" is an empty hash, while in actuality it should be a hash containing c1 => 10
The second record is as expected since it lacks a "b1" key.

It seems that this bug only occurs if a dot is in the fields specification.  If requesting a top-level key without a dot, it works fine:

require "rubygems"
require "mongo"
test_db = Mongo::Connection.new.db("test")
test_coll = test_db.collection("test")
test_coll.find({}, :fields => "a1").each do |doc|
  puts doc.inspect
end

prints:
{"_id"=>#<Mongo::ObjectID:0x118dafc @data=[74, 150, 149, 11, 9, 241, 25, 52, 98, 65, 127, 208]>, "a1"=>{"b1"=>{"c1"=>10.0}}}
{"_id"=>#<Mongo::ObjectID:0x118d6b0 @data=[74, 150, 149, 58, 9, 241, 25, 52, 98, 65, 127, 209]>, "a1"=>{"b2"=>{"c1"=>20.0}}}

summary: add Mongo::VERSION constant
RuntimeError under (concurrent) load
description:
I'm investigating an issue with the Ruby driver (on Rails and Sinatra). 
It only reveals itself when the web server is under significant load. 
I tried jnunemaker's MongoMapper as well as the activerecord-mongo-adapter.

All is well when testing with ab like this:

    ab -n 1000 http://my-ruby-project-with-mongodb/

However, increasing the concurrency to everything > 1 like ...

    ab -n 1000 -c 2 http://my-ruby-project-with-mongodb/

... leads to the following error within a few seconds:

summary: BSON.serialize on unsupported type segfaults instead of throwing an exception
description:
Attempting to serialize a structure containing a type BSON doesn't know to serialize creates a segmentation fault within the C extension.  This behavior extends to any attempt to insert a record into MongoDB using the Mongo Ruby driver if the hash key is a non-primitive Ruby object.

I believe that this operation should raise a Ruby exception so that developers can catch and respond to the error without their application crashing.

An IRB session follows demonstrating both a success case and a crash case:

    
[12:34][~/projects/aarweb(groups)]$ irb

>> require 'mongo'
=> true

>> BSON.serialize(:foo => 'bar')
=> #<ByteBuffer:0x00000100c768e8 @buf="\x12\x00\x00\x00\x02foo\x00\x04\x00\x00\x00bar\x00\x00", @cursor=18, @order=:little_endian, @int_pack_order="V", @double_pack_order="E">

>> o = Object.new
=> #<Object:0x00000100c8f900>

>> BSON.serialize(:object => o)

summary: Implement Min/Max Keys
checked out sockets in connection.rb are not checked in if the transaction is interrupted by an Exception
description:
If one has, for example:

SystemTimer::timeout(10)
  obj_cache.each do |obj|
   mongo_collection.save( obj )
  end
end

...and the Timeout is raised while a socket is checked out from the pool, it won't be available again. Thus there might be no more connections available.

patch attached.

summary: Type checking for Ruby time classes is overly strict
description:
Rails ActiveSupport comes with a number of classes to make date/time management in Ruby more flexible.  The 'duck typing' conventions of Ruby programming usually make it irrelevant whether a time object is an instance of type Time or a class that supports the same methods.  

The BSON conversion within the mongo-ruby driver breaks these conventions.  This creates particular difficulties for Rails developers who work with the config.time_zone parameter set, which causes most times to be cast into ActiveSupport::TimeWithZone.  This class, along with Date and DateTime and others, presents an exception "Mongo::InvalidDocument: Unsupported type for BSON (1)".

My suggestion would be to implement more testing in the bson_type method.  In addition to the === Time test that's already in the case statement, you could cast to Time when the object responds to 'to_time'.  Other types could be similarly tested for their to_* equivalents as well.  If that seems too murky, putting class checks against the common extension classes in the Ruby standard library and in ActiveSupport would be a good 80% solution.

description:
Trying to automate creating and deleting mongo database and user for it.

conn = Mongo::Connection.new("localhost")
conn.db("admin").authenticate("root", "password")
conn.db("foobar").eval("db.addUser()", "foo", "bar")

/var/lib/gems/1.8/gems/mongo-0.18.3/lib/../lib/mongo/db.rb:293:in `eval': eval failed: invoke failed: JS Error: ReferenceError: hex_md5 is not defined (anon):42 (Mongo::OperationFailure)
        from ./test-mongo.rb:15

Some another evaluations from ruby script works, like: 
conn.db("some_base").eval("db.dropDatabase()")

I sepose it's error from mongodb, but same evaluations in mongo shell works

Is this a bug or is there an another way to create mongodb user from ruby script?

Gem name "mongo-0.18.3p" screws up rubygems-1.3.4's ability to index the gem properly
description:
We keep an in-house rubygems repository, I added the mongo-0.18.3p gem, then reindexed the repo. When i did a gem list --remote --source=http://rubygems.internal that gem was not listed. I tried this same procedure with mongo-0.18.3.gem and the problem did not occur. This is using rubygems 1.3.4. 

The problem also seems to manifest itself in the gem documentation server, as the mongo documentation does not appear when using the *p gem.

summary: There doesn't appear to be a way to delete a GridStore file created in an alternate root collection
description:
Using the GridStore.open and GridStore.new methods, it's possible to specify a :root option, which allows one to change the default root collection where a file will be stored. I can't see a way through the GridStore.unlink class method to unlink a file in an alternate root collection.

It seems the only way to actually accomplish the deletion is to call GridStore.new(db, name, 'r', :root => 'root_name') and do what the 'unlink' method is doing "by hand."

I've included a little script that shows what i'm talking about. 

Perhaps the following would be an improvement?

def self.unlink(db, *names)
  opts = names.last.kind_of?(Hash) ? names.pop : {}

  names.each do |name|
    gs = GridStore.new(db, name, 'r', opts)
    gs.send(:delete_chunks)  # does this work in 1.9 ?
    gs.collection.remove('_id' => gs.files_id)
  end
end

Then you could do:

> GridStore.unlink(db, 'filename1', 'filename2', 'filename3', :root => 'alt_root')

Admittedly, the 'r' is kind of ugly, but this would have the desired effect and would also allow the method to keep its varargs signature.
